titleStmt.title: SII 1.67: original edition by Eugen Julius Theodor Hultzsch
No. 67. ON A PIECE OF ROCK ON THE TOP OF THE TIRUMALAI HILL.

summary.p: This inscription is dated in the 12th year of Ko-Parakesarivarman, alias U·∏çaiyƒÅr RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a-deva. It opens with a long list of the countries which the king had conquered. Among these we find ‚Äúthe seven and a half lakshas (of revenue) of Ira·π≠·π≠apƒÅ·∏çi,‚Äù which RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a took from Jayasi·πÅha. This conquest must have taken place between his 7th and 10th years, as another of RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a's inscriptions, which is dated in his 7th year,Madras Christian College Magazine, Vol. V, p. 41. does not mention it, while it occurs in some unpublished Tanjore inscriptions of the 10th year.The conquest of ‚Äúthe high mountains of Navanedikkula (?)‚Äù took also place between the 7th and 10th years, but subsequently to the war against Jayasi·πÅha. The Jayasi·πÅha of the present inscription can be no other than the Western ChƒÅlukya king Jayasi·πÅha III. (about ≈öaka 940 to about 964), who, according to the Miraj grant, ‚Äúwarred against the Chola,‚ÄùInd. Ant. Vol. VIII, p. 18. and who, in another inscription, is called ‚Äúthe lion to the elephant RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a.‚ÄùRƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a-gaja-m·πõigarƒÅja; Ind. Ant. Vol. V, p. 15, lines 13 f. Mr. Rice's Mysore Inscriptions, p. 149. Consequently, ‚Äúthe seven and a half lakshas. of Ira·π≠·π≠apƒÅ·∏çi‚Äù have to be taken as a designation of the Chalukyan empire,See the remarks of Sir W. Elliot in Carr's Seven Pagodas, pp. 138 ff. which, in two Eastern Chalukya grants, is called ‚Äúthe Dekhan which yields seven and a half lakshas.‚ÄùSee No. 39, line 26, and Ind. Ant. Vol. XIV, p. 51, line 29. As both RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a and Jayasi·πÅha III. boast of having conquered the other, it must be assumed that either the success was on both sides alternately, or that neither of the two obtained a lasting advantage. If, in order to identify RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a, the enemy of Jayasi·πÅha III., we turn to the table of the Eastern Chalukya Dynasty, which is found on page 32, above, we find that he cannot be that RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏ça, who reigned from ≈öaka 985 to 1034. Undoubtedly, the enemy of Jayasi·πÅha III. was that RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏ça of the S≈´ryava·πÅ≈õa, whose daughter Amma·πÖga-devƒ´ was married to the Eastern Chalukya king RƒÅjarƒÅja I.See p. 51, above. (≈öaka 944 to 985). He is further identical with that RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏ça, who was the son of RƒÅjarƒÅja of the S≈´ryava·πÅ≈õa, and whose younger sister K≈´ndavƒÅ was married to the Eastern Chalukya king VimalƒÅdityaInd. Ant. Vol. XIV, p. 50. (≈öaka 937 (?) to 944). From certain Tanjore inscriptions it can be safely concluded, that he was the successor of his father RƒÅjarƒÅja-deva, whose time I have tried to fix in the introduction of No. 40, above. RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a's name occurs also on the seal of the large Leyden grant, and he is in all probability identical with the MadhurƒÅntaka, i.e., ‚Äúthe destroyer of Madura,‚Äù who issued that grant after the death of his father RƒÅjarƒÅja.See lines 86 to 88 of the large Leyden grant; Dr. Burgess' Arch. Survey of S. India, Vol. IV, p. 208.

Among the other countries, which RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a is said to have conquered, the two first in the list are I·∏çaitu·πüai-nƒÅ·∏çu, i.e., the country of E·∏çatore, the head-quarters of a tƒÅlluqa in the Mais≈´r District, and Va·πâavƒÅ≈õi, i.e., BanawƒÅsi in the North Kanara District of the Bombay Presidency. With Ko·∏∑·∏∑ippƒÅkkai compare KollipƒÅke, which, according to Mr. Fleet,Kanarese Dynasties, p. 44. was one of the capitals of the Western ChƒÅlukya king Jayasi·πÅha III. ƒ™·πõam or ƒ™·πõa-ma·πá·∏çalam is Ceylon. ‚ÄúThe king of the South‚Äù (Te·πâ·πâava·πâ) is the PƒÅ·πá·∏çya king. Of him the inscription says, that he had formerly given the crown of Sundara to the king of Ceylon, from whom RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a took that crown of Sundara. The name Sundara occurs in the traditional lists of PƒÅ·πá·∏çya kings.Sewell's Lists of Antiquities, Vol. II, pp. 218 ff. Dr. Caldwell's Comparative Grammar, pp. 139 ff. of the Introduction and pp. 535 ff. In the present inscription, the term ‚Äúthe crown of Sundara‚Äù seems to be used in the sense of ‚Äúthe crown of the PƒÅ·πá·∏çya king,‚Äù and the composer of the historical part of the inscription seems to have known Sundara as a former famous member of the PƒÅ·πá·∏çya dynasty. But no conclusions as to the date of Sundara can be drawn from this mention of his name. The names of the PƒÅ·πá·∏çya king, who was conquered by the king of Ceylon, and of the king of Ceylon, who was conquered by RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a, are not mentioned. The inscription further records that RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a vanquished the Kera·∏∑a, i.e., the king of Malabar. With ≈öakkara-ko·π≠·π≠am, whose king Vikrama-Vƒ´ra was defeated by RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a, compare Chakrako·π≠a, whose lord was conquered by the Western ChƒÅlukya king VikramƒÅditya VI.,Dr. Bu7hler's VikramƒÅ·πÖkacharita, sarga iv, verse 30. and Chakrago·π≠·π≠a, which was taken by the Hoysa·∏∑a king Vish·πáuvardhana.Mr. Fleet's Kanarese Dynasties, p. 66. Madura-ma·πá·∏çalam is the PƒÅ·πá·∏çya country, the capital of which was Madura. O·∏ç·∏çavishaya, the country of the O·∏ç·∏ças or O·∏çrasSanskrit: O·∏çra; Telugu: ≈å·∏çhrulu, the inhabitants of ≈å·∏çhra-dƒì≈õa; Kanarese: O·∏ç·∏çaru; Tamil: o·π≠·π≠ar or o·π≠·π≠iyar, the inhabitants of o·π≠·π≠am or o·π≠·π≠iyam. and the U-cha of Hiuen-Tsiang,Beal's Si-yu-ki, Vol. II, p. 204. is the modern Orissa. Ko≈õalai-nƒÅ·∏çu is Southern Kosala, the Kiao-sa-lo of Hiuen-Tsiang,Ibid. p. 209. which, according to General Cunningham, corresponds to the upper valley of the MahƒÅnadƒ´ and its tributaries.Arch. Survey of India, Vol. XVII, p. 68. Takka·πáalƒÅ·∏çam and UttiralƒÅ·∏çam are Northern and Southern LƒÅ·π≠a (GujarƒÅt). The former was taken from a certain Ra·πáa≈õ≈´ra. Further, RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a asserts that he conquered Va·πÖgƒÅ·∏∑a-de≈õa, i.e., Bengal, from a certain Govindachandra and extended his operations as far as the Ga·πÖgƒÅ. The remaining names of countries and kings I have been unable to identify.

The inscription mentions Tirumalai, i.e., ‚Äúthe holy mountain,‚Äù and records a gift to the temple on its top, which was called Kundavai-JinƒÅlaya, i.e., the Jina temple of Kundavai. According to an Eastern Chalukya grantInd. Ant. Vol. XIV, p. 53, lines 60 f. (and an unpublished Chidambaram inscriptionSee paragraph 5 of my Progress Report for February, March and April 1888, Madras G.O., 27th July 1888, No. 745, Public. ), K≈´ndavƒÅ (or Kundavai) was the name of the daughter of RƒÅjarƒÅja of the S≈´ryava·πÅ≈õa, the younger sister of RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a, and the queen of the Eastern Chalukya king VimalƒÅditya. The Tanjore inscriptions mention another, still earlier Kundavai, who was the daughter of the Cho·∏∑a king ParƒÅntaka II., the elder sister of the Cho·∏∑a king RƒÅjarƒÅja-deva, and the queen of the Pallava king Vandyadeva.See paragraph 8 of my Progress Report for July, August and September 1888, Madras G.O., 7th November 1888, No. 1050, Public. It seems very probable that it was one of these two queens, viz., either the younger sister or the aunt of the then reigning sovereign RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a, who founded the temple on the top of the Tirumalai Rock and called it after herself. As Tirumalai is much closer to the Pallava country, than to the country of the Eastern Chalukyas, we shall scarcely be wrong in attributing the foundation of the temple rather to the king's aunt, who was a Pallava queen, than to his younger sister, who was married to an Eastern Chalukya king.

According to this and the next inscription, the village at the foot of the Tirumalai Hill bore the name of Vaigav≈´r and belonged to Mugai-nƒÅ·∏çu, a division of Pa·πÖga·∏∑anƒÅ·∏çu, which formed part of Jaya·πÖko·πá·∏ça-Cho·∏∑a-ma·πá·∏çalam.

With the text of the subjoined inscription, I have compared four other inscriptions of RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a, viz., 1. the inscription No. 68, which is likewise dated in the 12th year; 2. an undated inscription of the KailƒÅsanƒÅtha Temple at Uttaramall≈´r in the Chingleput District, an impression of which I owe to the kindness of Mr. R.Sewell; 3. an inscription of the B·πõihadƒ´≈õvara Temple at Tanjore (15th year); and 4. an inscription of the Chidambaram Temple (24th year).

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)
Conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
svasti ≈õrƒ´ . tiruma·πâ·πâi va·∏∑araviru nilama·π≠antaiyum porccayappƒÅvaiyu√±cƒ´rtta·πâiccelviyunta·πâ perunteviyarƒÅki I·πâpu·πüu≈çther inscriptions read i·πâpu·πüa. ne·π≠u tiyalAn inscription at Uttaramall≈´r reads t≈´yar. ≈™·∏ªiyu·∏∑ I·π≠aitu
·πüainƒÅ·π≠untu·π≠ar va·πâavelippa·π≠ar va·πâavƒÅciyu√±cu·∏∑·∏∑icc≈´·∏ª mati·π≠ko·∏∑·∏∑ippƒÅkkaiyuna·πá·πáa·πükaru mura·πáThe ra of mura·πá looks almost like ka. ma·πá·πáaikka·π≠akkamum poru ka·π≠al ƒ™·∏ªattaracar tamu·π≠iyum ƒÄ·πÖka
var teviyaro·πÖke·∏ªi·πâmu·π≠iyumu·πâ·πâavar pakkaltte·πâ·πâavar vaitta cuntaramu·π≠iyum IntiranƒÅramunte·πá·π≠irai ƒ™·∏ªama·πá·π≠alamu·∏ªuvatum E·πüi pa·π≠aikkera·∏∑ar
mu·πüaimaiyi·πüc≈´·π≠u·πÖkulata·πâamƒÅkiya palar puka·∏ª mu·π≠iyu√±ce·πÖkatir mƒÅlaiyu√±ca·πÖkatir velaittol peru·πÖkƒÅva·πüpala pa·∏ªantivu√±ceruvi·πüce·πâa
vilƒÅ ·π≠anjore inscription reads ci·πâavi for ce·πâavil. Irupattoru kƒÅlaraicuka·∏∑ai ka·π≠·π≠a paracurƒÅma·πâ mevaru√±cƒÅntimattivavara·πá karuti Iruttiya cem po·πü·πüiruttaku mu·π≠iyum paya·πÖko·π≠u pa·∏ªi mika muca·πÖkiyil mu
tuki·π≠·π≠o·∏∑itta cayaci·πÖka·πâci looks like cƒ´. A·∏∑apperumA Tanjore inscription reads a·∏∑apparum. puka·∏ªo·π≠um pƒ´·π≠iyalAn inscription at Uttaramall≈´r reads pi·π≠i instead of pƒ´·π≠iyal. Ira·π≠·π≠apƒÅ·π≠i E·∏ªarai Ilakkamunavanetikkulapperumalaika·∏∑um vikkiramavƒ´rar cakkarako·π≠·π≠amu
mutirapa·π≠avallai maturama·πá·π≠alamum kƒÅmi·π≠aiva·∏∑aiyanƒÅma·πáaikko·πáamum ve√±cilaivƒ´rar pa√±cappa·∏∑·∏∑iyum pƒÅcu·π≠ai≈çther inscriptions read pƒÅca·π≠ai. ppa·∏ªana·πâmƒÅcu·πáitecamum Ayarvi
l va·πá kirttiyƒÅtinakaravaiyi·πücan=tira·πâ·πüol kulattiratara·πâai vi·∏∑aiyamarkka·∏∑attukki·∏∑aiyo·π≠um pi·π≠ittuppala ta·πâatto·π≠u ni·πüai kulata·πâakkuvai
yu√±ci·π≠·π≠aru√±The Uttaramall≈´r and Tanjore inscriptions read ki·π≠·π≠aru√±. ce·πüi mi·∏∑aiyo·π≠·π≠aA Chidambaram inscription reads pu·πâalo·π≠·π≠a instead of mi·∏∑aiyo·π≠·π≠a. vi·π£aiyamum p≈´curar cer nalkkocalainƒÅ·π≠u- n=ta·πâmapƒÅla·πâai vem mu·πâaiya·∏ªittu va·πá·π≠u·πüai colaitta·πá·π≠ayuttiThe Chidambaram and ·π≠anjore inscriptions read putti. yumira·πáa
c≈´ra·πâai mura·πáu·πüattƒÅkkittikka·πáai kirttittakka·πáalƒÅ·π≠amu·πÖkovin=tacan=ta·πâ mƒÅvi·∏ªi- n=to·π≠atta·πÖkƒÅta cƒÅral va·πÖkƒÅ·∏∑atecamun=to·π≠u ka·π≠a·πüca·πÖkuko·π≠·π≠a·πâ mahƒ´pƒÅla·πâai
ve√±camaThe inscription No. 68 reads camar for cama. va·∏∑ƒÅkatta√±cuvittaru·∏∑i O·πá·π≠i·πüal yƒÅ·πâaiyum pe·πá·π≠ir pa·πá·π≠ƒÅramunittilane·π≠u·πÖka·π≠aluttiralƒÅ·π≠amum ve·πüiAn Uttaramall≈´r inscription reads e·πüi. ma·πáa·πü·πüirttatte·πüi pu·πâa·πüka·πÖkaiyumƒÅp
poru ta·πá·π≠ƒÅ·πüko·πá·π≠a kopparakecaripa·πâmarƒÅ·πâa U·π≠aiyƒÅr ≈õrƒ´rƒÅjendraco·∏∑adeva·πüku yƒÅ·πá·π≠u 10 2 ƒÄvatu [ja]ya·πÖko·πá·π≠aco·∏ªama·πá·π≠alattu pa·πÖka·∏∑anƒÅ·π≠·π≠u na·π≠uvil
vakai mukainƒÅ·π≠·π≠uppa·∏∑·∏∑iccan=tam vaikav≈´rttirumalai ≈õrƒ´kun=tavaijinƒÅlayattu deva·πükupperumpƒÅ·πáappƒÅ·π≠ikkaraiva·∏ªimalliy≈´r Irukkum vyƒÅ
pƒÅri nannappaya·πâ ma·πáavƒÅ·π≠·π≠i cƒÅmu·πá·π≠appai vaitta tirunan=tƒÅvi·∏∑akku . O·πâ·πüinukkukkƒÅcu Irupatum tiruvamutukku vaitta kƒÅcu pattum .The remainder of the inscription is written in faint and small characters, and seems to be a rough postscript, which was added after the bulk of the inscription had been carefully engraved by a skilled stonemason.

Hail! Prosperity! In the 12th year of (the reign of) Ko-Parakesarivarman, alias U·∏çaiyƒÅr ≈örƒ´-RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a-deva, who,‚Äîduring his long life (which resembled that of) pure people, (and in which) the great goddess of the earth, the goddess of victory in battle, and the beautiful and matchless goddess of fortune, who had become his great queens, gave him pleasure, while (his own) illustrious queen was prospering,‚Äîconquered with (his) great and warlike army I·∏çaitu·πüai-nƒÅ·∏çu; Va·πâavƒÅ≈õi, the roads (to which are bounded by) continuous walls of trees; Ko·∏∑·∏∑ippƒÅkkai, whose walls are surrounded by ≈õu·∏∑·∏∑i (trees); Ma·πá·πáaikka·∏çakkam of unapproachable strength; the crown of the king of ƒ™·πõam, (which is situated in the midst of) the rough sea; the exceedingly beautiful crown of the queen of the king of that (country); the crown of Sundara, which the king of the South (i.e., the PƒÅ·πá·∏çya) had formerly given to that (king of ƒ™·πõam); the pearl-necklace of Indra; the whole ƒ™·πõa-ma·πá·∏çalam on the transparent sea; the crown praised by many, a family-treasure, which the spearthrowing (king of) Kera·∏∑a usually wore; the garland of the sun (?); many ancient islands, which are the old and great guards of the shore, against which conches are dashed; the crown of pure gold, worthy of Lakshmƒ´, which Para≈õurƒÅma, who, out of anger, bound the kings twenty-one times in battle, having thought the fort of the island of ≈öƒÅndima (i.e., ≈öƒÅntimat ?) unapproachable, had deposited (there); the seven and a half lakshas of Ira·π≠·π≠a-pƒÅ·∏çi‚Äîthrough the conquest of which immeasurable fame arose‚Äî(of) Jayasi·πÅha, who, out of fear and full of revenge, turned his back at Mu≈õa·πÖgi (?) and hid himself; the high mountains of Navanedikkula; ≈öakkara-ko·π≠·π≠am (belonging to) Vikrama-Vƒ´ra; Madura-ma·πá·∏çalam with the fort of Mudira-pa·∏ça (?); NƒÅma·πáaikko·πáam, which is surrounded by dense groves; Pa√±chappa·∏∑·∏∑i (belonging to) Ve√±jilai-Vƒ´raLiterally, ‚Äúthe hero of the cruel bow.‚Äù Compare ve√±cilaiccelva·πâ, an epithet of Vƒ´rabhadra as bearing a cruel bow (Winslow). ; the good MƒÅ≈õu·πái-de≈õa, where leaves and fruits are green; the large heap of family-treasures, together with many (other) treasures, (which he carried away) after having seized Dhƒ´ratara of the old race of the moon, together with his family, in a fight which took place in the hall (at) ƒÄdinagar, (a city) which is famous for its unceasing abundance; O·∏ç·∏ça-vishaya, whose copious waters are difficult to approach; the good Ko≈õalai-nƒÅ·∏çu, where BrƒÅhma·πáas assemble; Da·πá·∏çabutti (i.e., Da·πá·∏ça-bhukti), in whose gardens bees abound, (and which he acquired) after having destroyed DharmapƒÅla in a hot battle; Takka·πáalƒÅ·∏çam (i.e., Dakshi·πáa-LƒÅ·π≠a), whose fame reaches (all) directions, (and which he occupied) after having forcibly attacked Ra·πáa≈õ≈´ra; Va·πÖgƒÅ·∏∑a-de≈õa, where the rain does not last (long), and from which Govindachandra, having lost his fortune, fled; elephants of rare strength, (which he took away) after having been pleased to frighten in a hot battle Mahƒ´pƒÅla of ≈öa·πÖgu-ko·π≠·π≠am (?), which touches the sea; the treasures of women (?); UttiralƒÅ·∏çam (i.e., Uttara-LƒÅ·π≠a) on the great sea of pearls; and the Ga·πÖgƒÅ, whose waters sprinkle tƒ´rthas on the burning sand:‚Äî

ChƒÅmu·πá·∏çappai, the wife of the merchant Nannappaya·πâ, who lives at PerumbƒÅ·πáappƒÅ·∏çi, (alias) Karaiva·πõi-malliy≈´r, gave a tirunandƒÅ lamp to the temple (called) Srƒ´-Kundavai-JinƒÅlaya (on) the holy mountain (Tirumalai) (at) the pa·∏∑·∏∑ichchandamThis term probably means ‚Äúa village belonging to a temple,‚Äù ‚Äúa holy village;‚Äù see p. 91, note 5. of Vaigav≈´r in Mugai-nƒÅ·∏çu, a division in the middle of Pa·πÖga·∏∑a-nƒÅ·∏çu, (which forms part) of Jaya·πÖko·πá·∏ça-≈öo·πõa-ma·πá·∏çalam.
Twenty kƒÅ≈õus were given for one (lamp) and ten kƒÅ≈õus for the sacred food.
Digital edition of SII 1.67 by converted to DHARMA conventions by Emmanuel Francis.
